design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore interconnected American overworld, encounter random enemies, engage in turn-based menu combat, collect items and restore health via medication/hospitals, progress through supernatural mystery by recruiting party members and unlocking psychic abilities
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Subversion of RPG genre expectations through modern-day American setting parody instead of fantasy; tension between familiar RPG mechanics and deliberately off-kilter, mundane subject matter (baseball bats vs swords, toy guns vs assault weapons, hospitals vs magic)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Young American boy discovering and mastering latent psychic powers to solve paranormal mystery afflicting the country; party-based adventure with contemporary American aesthetic
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately avoids both pure fantasy and pure sci-fi despite containing both; parodies Western culture and Americana; uses humble, everyday weapons and modern infrastructure (trains, ATMs, phones) instead of medieval tropes; all party members are children; employs oblique projection art style at designer's specific request; treats mundane American locations (warehouses, laboratories) as dungeons

difficulty.checkpointing: Game save via phone mechanic allows persistent progress checkpointing
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Limited player agency; difficulty noted as polarizing critics, with balance issues reducing accessibility; no documented difficulty select or adaptive systems

progression.upgrade_system: Party member recruitment (Ninten, Lloyd, Ana, Teddy); psychic ability unlocking for Ninten and Ana; weapon and item acquisition; HP/PP restoration through medication and hospital visits
progression.unlock_mechanism: Story-driven party member recruitment; psychic power learning through story progression; interconnected world map accessed via trains between towns
replayability_hooks: Item storage system with twins at home creates inventory management puzzle
Banking system via ATM for money management strategy
Multiple weapon/armor options provide loadout variation
Psychic ability combinations create different tactical approaches

world_building_techniques: Interconnected overworld that does not segment locations—all areas connect in one continuous game world
Encounter design parodies Americana: hippies, zombies, possessed objects, robots, mind-controlled humans and animals, extraterrestrials
Environmental storytelling through location types: warehouses, laboratories, trains, hospitals instead of fantasy dungeons and castles
Modern infrastructure as gameplay elements: phones for saving, ATMs for banking, trains for travel
First-person battle perspective with menu-based combat mimics Dragon Quest but in contemporary setting
Party member personalities tied to their roles and abilities

faction_indicators: Mind-controlled humans and animals (loss of individual agency)
Extraterrestrial forces (external threat to human civilization)
danger_signals: Paranormal phenomena spiraling the country into disarray (systemic threat)
Possessed objects and vehicles (corruption of mundane life)
environmental_storytelling: Late 20th-century American towns as setting reflects specific cultural moment
Warehouse and laboratory locations instead of castles signal industrial/scientific intrusion
Baseball bats and toy guns as weapons reflect childhood/Americana aesthetic
Trains as travel method create interconnected American landscape
Hospitals and medication systems as core health recovery reflect modern infrastructure
